coredata.dc:title: An off-on fluorescence method for acid phosphatase assay based on the inner filter effect of MnO2 nanosheets on vitamin B2
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Li
Qin, Shangying
Wen, Chuang
Xu, Yuanjin
Lin, Zhongwei
Wang, Yilin
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence analysis has attracted much attention due to its rapidity and sensitivity. The present work describes a novel fluorescence detection method for acid phosphatase (ACP) on the basis of inner-filter effect (IFE), where MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) and vitamin B2 (VB2) are served as absorbers and fluorophores, respectively. In the absence of ACP, the absorption band of MnO2 NSs overlaps well with the excitation band of VB2, resulting in effective IFE and inhibition of VB2 fluorescence. In the presence of ACP, 2-phospho-L-ascorbic acid trisodium salt (AAP) is hydrolyzed to generate ascorbic acid (AA), which efficiently trigger the reduction of MnO2 NSs into Mn2+ ions, causing the weakening of the MnO2 NSs absorption band and the recovery of VB2 fluorescence. Further investigation indicates that the fluorescence recovery degree of VB2 increases with the increase of ACP concentration. Under selected experimental conditions, the proposed method can achieve sensitive detection of ACP in the ranges of 0.5–4.0 mU/mL and 4.0–15 mU/mL along with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.14 mU/mL. Finally, this method was successfully applied for the detection of ACP in human serum samples with satisfactory recoveries in the range of 95.0 %-108 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Vitamin B2
MnO2 nanosheets
Fluorescence
Inner filter effect
Determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quenching and recovery of VB2 fluorescence
Detection of ACP with VB2-MnO2 NSs as probe
Analysis of ACP in serum sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (YCSW2023049).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of MnO2 NSs. Inset: UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs; (B) XPS spectrum of MnO2 NSs; (C) Deconstruction spectrum of Mn 2p, (D) Deconstruction spectrum of O 1s.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of VB2, VB2 + MnO2 NSs, VB2 + MnO2 NSs + AAP, VB2 + MnO2 NSs + ACP, VB2 + MnO2 NSs + AAP + ACP; (B) Normalized excitation, emission spectra of VB2 and absorption spectra of MnO2 NSs; (C) Fluorescence decay curves of VB2 in the absence and presence of MnO2 NSs; (D) TEM image of MnO2 NSs in the presence of ACP and AAP.
(A) The fluorescence spectra of 5 μM VB2 in the presence of various MnO2 NSs concentrations; effects of (B) pH value; (C) reaction time; and (D) AAP concentration on the fluorescence recovery.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of the VB2-MnO2 NSs probe with various concentrations of ACP. (B) Calibration curve of the assay system for ACP detection.
The selectivity of VB2-MnO2 NSs system to ACP (ACP is 10 mU/mL, ALP, Gox, and Uox are 50 mU/mL, GSH and Cys are 50 μM, NEM is 250 μM, and other substances are 500 μM).
Schematic illustration of fluorescence sensor for ACP based on MnO2 NSs -VB2 probe.
Comparison of different ACP determination methods.
Measurements of ACP in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: An off-on fluorescence method for acid phosphatase assay based on the inner filter effect of MnO2 nanosheets on vitamin B2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530003, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence analysis has attracted much attention due to its rapidity and sensitivity. The present work describes a novel fluorescence detection method for acid phosphatase (ACP) on the basis of inner-filter effect (IFE), where MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) and vitamin B2 (VB2) are served as absorbers and fluorophores, respectively. In the absence of ACP, the absorption band of MnO2 NSs overlaps well with the excitation band of VB2, resulting in effective IFE and inhibition of VB2 fluorescence. In the presence of ACP, 2-phospho-L-ascorbic acid trisodium salt (AAP) is hydrolyzed to generate ascorbic acid (AA), which efficiently trigger the reduction of MnO2 NSs into Mn2+ ions, causing the weakening of the MnO2 NSs absorption band and the recovery of VB2 fluorescence. Further investigation indicates that the fluorescence recovery degree of VB2 increases with the increase of ACP concentration. Under selected experimental conditions, the proposed method can achieve sensitive detection of ACP in the ranges of 0.5–4.0 mU/mL and 4.0–15 mU/mL along with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.14 mU/mL. Finally, this method was successfully applied for the detection of ACP in human serum samples with satisfactory recoveries in the range of 95.0 %-108 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acid phosphatase (ACP), as a phosphomonoesterase, is extensively present in various mammalian tissues and body fluids, which can be acidic hydrolyzed into orthophosphate monoesters and release inorganic phosphates [1,2]. The imbalance of ACP levels is considered to be closely related to various diseases, such as multiple myeloma, Gaucher's disease, hyperparathyroidism, prostate disease, hepatitis, etc [3–6]. Therefore, accurate determination of ACP levels in blood is of great significance for disease diagnosis. So far, several available techniques for quantitative determination of ACP have been explored, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], electrochemistry [8,9], colorimetry [10], fluorometry [11], and so on. Undeniably, each method has its own advantages and disadvantages. Fluorometry is highly favored due to its advantages of high sensitivity, fast response, and simple operation. In the past two decades, with the increasing development of synthesis technology, lots of fluorescent materials, such as precious metal nanoclusters [11], luminescent quantum dots [12,13], fluorescent polymers [14], and fluorescent dyes [15], have been applied for the detection of ACP in biological systems.

Manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs), a layered transition metal oxide nanomaterial with redox activity, has been widely used in the field of biosensing [16–18] because of its simple preparation, strong oxidation ability, excellent catalytic activity, high specific surface area, broad absorption band, and large molar extinction coefficient. The absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs cover both the ultraviolet and visible regions (200–600 nm), with a molar extinction coefficient of up to 9.6 × 103 M−1 cm−1 at 380 nm [19]. Such a wide absorption spectrum almost overlaps with the excitation or emission spectra of most fluorescent materials. Therefore, MnO2 NSs can achieve effective fluorescence quenching through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [20] or inner filter effect (IFE) [21]. On the other hand, MnO2 NSs is an oxidizing nanomaterial that is easily decomposed by the reduction of reducing substances. As a fluorescent quenching agent with oxidation ability, MnO2 NSs are often used to construct off–on, off–on-off, and ratio type fluorescence sensing systems for determining uric acid [22], ascorbic acid (AA) [23], glutathione [24,25], and their related analytes (alkaline phosphatase [26], butyrylcholinesterase [21], organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) [27], etc.). For examples, the introduction of uric acid in carbon dots (CDs)-MnO2 NSs fluorescence quenching system can lead to the reduction and decomposition of MnO2 NSs, as well as the restoration of CDs fluorescence. Based on these, Amjadi et al. established an off–on fluorescence method for uric acid assay [22]. Cai et al. also developed a turn-on fluorescence assay for glutathione detection using CDs and MnO2 NSs [25]. The MnO2 NSs-induced CDs fluorescence quenching can be recovered by the addition of butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) and acetylthiocholine. OPs, an inhibitor of BChE activity, can decrease the decomposition of MnO2 NSs and lead to fluorescence quenching again. On the basis of these phenomena, the off–on-off fluorescence sensing platform for OPs detection was constructed by Yan’s group [27]. The introduction of AA into MnO2 NSs-modulated gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) and thiamine (VB1) fluorescence system results in an increase and a decrease in their fluorescence, respectively. This ratio fluorescence sensing technology has been used for the sensitive determination of AA [20]. Obviously, the aforementioned fluorescence sensing systems involve two types of nanomaterials, namely MnO2 NSs and CDs (or Au NCs).

Vitamin B2 (VB2), also known as riboflavin, is a water-soluble and fluorescent vitamin, which plays an important role in energy metabolism in biological systems and human diet. Although there have been reports on the ratio fluorescence determination of VB2 using perylene [28], graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [29], or silicon nanoparticle (Si NPs) [30] as donors and VB2 as acceptors based on FRET, there have been no reports on the determination of other analytes using VB2 as a fluorescent reporter. In this work, an IFE-based fluorescence probe composed of MnO2 NSs and VB2 was constructed for turn-on detection of ACP, in which MnO2 NSs and VB2 serve as quencher and fluorescence reporter, respectively (Scheme 1 ). When excited at 370 nm, VB2 exhibits its maximum emission wavelength at 527 nm. Owing to the strong absorption of MnO2 NSs at 370 nm, the presence of MnO2 NSs weakens the intensity of excitation light, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence of VB2 through IFE. However, MnO2 NSs can be reduced by AA generated from ACP-catalyzed hydrolysis of 2-phospho-L-ascorbic acid trisodium salt (AAP) to Mn2+, causing a decrease in its absorption and a recovery of VB2 fluorescence. Thus, a fluorescence turn-on strategy for ACP detection based on IFE mechanism has been achieved.

ACP, AAP, alkaline phosphatase (ALP), glucose oxidase (Gox), uricase (Uox), L-cysteine (Cys), and acetic acid (CH3COOH) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China. Glutathione (GSH), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), potassium chloride (KCl), N-Ethylmaleimide (NEM), manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2‧4H2O), calcium chloride (CaCl2), and magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), glucose (Glu), and glutamate (Gla), histidine (His), lysine (Lys), methionine (Met), phenylalanine (Phe), and valine (Vla) were acquired from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China.

The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu 5301PC fluorescence spectrometer and a Unico-4802 spectrophotometer, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a K-Alpha spectrometer. The morphology of MnO2 NSs was demonstrated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2800) analysis.

The synthesis of MnO2 NSs refers to the previous publication with slight modifications [31]. In brief, 10 mL of MnCl2 (0.3 M) was transferred to a 20 mL of TMA⋅OH (0.6 M) and H2O2 (3 %) in their mixture. After continuous stirring at room temperature for 12 h, the resulting suspension was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 10 min), and the precipitate was washed with double deionized water (DDW) and methanol three times, respectively. Subsequently, the purified MnO2 NSs was redispersed in DDW, and an ultrasonic treatment was performed to prepare a uniform MnO2 NSs stock solution. According to the Lamber-Beer law and the ε value of 9.6 × 103 M−1 cm−1 at 380 nm [19], the accurate concentration of MnO2 NSs in solution was calculated.

Series of test solutions were prepared by mixing 200 μL of AAP (5 mM) and 200 μL of ACP at different concentrations, which were incubated at 37 °C for 40 min [32] followed by the addition of 800 μL of MnO2 NSs (250 μM), 200 μL of VB2 (50 μM), and 600 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0). After incubation at room temperature for another 5 min, the fluorescence spectrum corresponding to different ACP concentrations excited at 370 nm was recorded. The fluorescence recovery value at 527 nm was defined as ΔF (ΔF = F - F 0, where F and F 0 denote the fluorescence intensity of detection system with and without ACP, respectively), it was used as the dependent variable for standard curve and evaluation criterion for optimizing detection conditions. All data were measured three times in parallel.

Several characterization tests were used to prove the successful synthesis of MnO2 NSs. The UV–visible absorption spectrum (inset in Fig. 1 A) exhibits a wide absorption band with a center wavelength of 380 nm that arises from the d-d transition of Mn (IV) in the octahedral [MnO6] unit [33]. The MnO2 NSs colloidal solution exhibits good stability, even after being stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 2 weeks, its absorption peak position and absorbance measurements are very consistent with previous measurements. TEM image (Fig. 1A) discloses that the sample has the characteristic two-dimensional morphology of MnO2 NSs, which is composed of ultra-thin structures with plenty of folds and wrinkles [34,35]. As shown in Fig. 1B, three peaks belonging to C 1s (285.08 eV), O 1s (530.08 eV) and Mn 2p (642.08 eV) are revealed by XPS survey in the full scan spectrum. In the deconstruction Mn 2p spectrum (Fig. 1C), the two prominent main peaks at 653.38 and 641.58 eV ascribed to Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 of MnO2 NSs [33,36] are observed. By deconvolution of the Mn 2p spectrum, a pair of peaks located at binding energy values of 653.68 and 643.08 eV are attributed to Mn4+, and another pair of peaks located at binding energies 652.78 and 641.48 eV are assigned to Mn4+ near the oxygen vacancy, which are very consistent with the XPS mode of MnO2 and indicate a tetravalence oxidation state for Mn [32,37]. The deconstruction O 1s spectrum can be separated into three peaks located at 528.88, 530.28, and 531.98 eV, corresponding to Mn-O, CO, and C-O, respectively [38]. All these validate the successful synthesis of MnO2 NSs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching and recovery of VB2 fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of VB2 in different systems were recorded. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, VB2 solution alone exhibits strong fluorescence, with an emission peak located at 527 nm (black line). The fluorescence of VB2 solution containing MnO2 NSs significantly decreases at 527 nm (red line), indicating that MnO2 NSs can quench the fluorescence of VB2. Introducing only AAP or ACP in the VB2-MnO2 NSs system will not cause any changes in the fluorescence spectrum (blue and green lines), implying that no chemical reaction has occurred between them. However, the simultaneous presence of AAP and ACP in the VB2-MnO2 NSs system results in a significant enhancement of fluorescence at 527 nm (purple line), suggesting that the fluorescence of VB2 quenched by MnO2 NSs has been restored.

As shown in Fig. 2B, VB2 exhibits excitation peaks at 370 and 450 nm, respectively. MnO2 NSs shows a broad absorption band centered at around 380 nm, which overlaps extensively with the excitation spectrum of VB2, and only overlaps slightly with the emission spectrum of VB2. Considering that the fluorescence spectrum of VB2 excited at 370 nm was recorded, we believe that the quenching of VB2 fluorescence is attributed to IFE. As depicted in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence decay curve for VB2 in the absence and presence of MnO2 NSs fits well with the double exponential decay function, and the calculated average fluorescence lifetimes are 4.89 ns and 4.92 ns, respectively. No significant changes in the average fluorescence lifetime of VB2 are observed before and after the addition of MnO2 NSs, further confirming that charge or energy transfer is not involved in the fluorescence quenching process [32]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of MnO2 NSs on VB2 is most likely to be IFE. Compared with its original morphology (Fig. 1A), the lamellar structure of MnO2 NSs is completely destroyed in the presence of AAP and ACP (Fig. 2D). Indicating that ACP catalyzed AAP hydrolysis triggers the decomposition of MnO2 NSs, and the chemical reaction of this process can be described as follows:

Based on the principle that ACP catalyzes the hydrolysis of AAP to produce AA which reduce MnO2 NSs to Mn2+, causing the fluorescence recovery of VB2 quenched by MnO2 NSs, a “turn-on” fluorescence method for ACP detection can be established by using VB2-MnO2 NSs as a probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ACP with VB2-MnO2 NSs as probe
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the best signal response, the effects of MnO2 NSs concentration on fluorescence quenching of VB2, pH value of HAc-NaAc buffer, reaction time between AA and MnO2 NSs, and AAP concentration in analysis solution on fluorescence recovery were systematically investigated. In VB2-MnO2 NSs systems with the same VB2 concentration, lower MnO2 NSs concentrations lead to higher background fluorescence and a narrower linear response range, while higher concentrations of MnO2 NSs result in lower background fluorescence and lower sensitivity. As shown in Fig. 3 A, 100 μM MnO2 NSs can quench 5 μM VB2 fluorescence by about 80 %, providing a wider fluorescence recovery range and higher sensitivity to ACP. Thus, a probe solution containing 5 μM VB2 and 100 μM MnO2 NSs was selected for further experiments. Due to the involvement of H+ in the redox reaction between AA and MnO2 NSs (eq.1), the influence of pH on ΔF was studied by using HAc-NaAc buffer with different pH values (2.5–5.0). As displayed in Fig. 3B, ΔF reaches its maximum value at pH 4.0, which was adopted for further investigations. As can be seen in Fig. 3C, the redox reaction between AA generated by ACP catalyzed hydrolysis of AAP and MnO2 NSs can be quickly completed in a short time, within 2 to 8 min, the change in ΔF value can be almost negligible. In subsequent experiments, 5 min was conservatively chosen as the reaction time for AA and MnO2 NSs. The influence of AAP concentration ranging from 0.2 to 0.8 mM on ΔF was also examined, and the results are depicted in Fig. 3D. As seen, with the increase of AAP concentration, the ΔF value increases progressively. Whereas the AAP concentration exceeds 0.5 mM, the ΔF value remains relatively stable. Thus, 0.5 mM was chosen as the ideal AAP concentration for the subsequent experiments.

Under the chosen experimental conditions, the linear response range, detection limit, and selectivity of the method were all assessed. The ACP concentration dependent fluorescence spectra of VB2-MnO2 NSs system are shown in Fig. 4 A. As observed, the fluorescence of VB2 gradually recovers as ACP concentration increases. Fig. 4B shows that the fluorescence recovery value at 527 nm (ΔF) exhibits good linear relationships with ACP concentration over the ranges of 0.5–4.0 mU/mL and 4.0–15 mU/mL, and the regression analysis of the data yields the following equations: ΔF = 21.60C ACP + 4.923 (R2 = 0.9948) and ΔF = 49.48C ACP − 137.0 (R2 = 0.9989). According to the 3σ/slope law (where σ is the standard deviation of 11 blank solution measurements), the limit of detection (LOD) for the low concentration range was estimated to be 0.14 mU/mL, showing high sensitivity for ACP detection. The relative standard deviations (RSD) for the five repeated tests of 3.0, 9.0, and 15.0 μM ACP were 1.0 %, 1.2 %, and 0.7 % respectively, suggesting a good precision of this approach. As summarized in Table 1 , the determination of ACP using VB2-MnO2 NSs as a probe exhibits better analytical performance in either linear range or LOD compared to other previously reported ACP detection techniques. Furthermore, this method does not use any precious metals, heavy metals, or toxic organic fluorescent dyes, and only requires the involvement of VB2 and MnO2 NSs, which is low-cost and environmentally friendly. All these suggest that the VB2-MnO2 NSs is an ideal probe for fluorescence monitoring of ACP content.

Considering that the anti-interference is very important for real sample detection, the selectivity of “turn on” fluorescence determination of ACP was evaluated with 15 kinds of interfering substances (Glu, ALP, Gox, Uox, Cys, GSH, Gla, His, Lys, Met, Phe, Val, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+). As exhibited in Fig. 5 , except for ACP, Cys, and GSH, which cause a significant fluorescence enhancement, no other interfering substances have obvious fluorescence response. However, the interference from Cys and GSH can be shielded with NEM [45]. Suggesting that the VB2-MnO2 NSs probe can be applied for determining ACP in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ACP in serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the availability of the VB2-MnO2 NSs probe, ACP detection was carried out in serum samples provided by adult volunteers in our team. To remove serum protein, serum sample and trichloroacetic acid (5 %) of equal volume were fully mixed, followed by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min). The supernatant was diluted 20-fold, and 100 μL of diluted sample was taken for ACP determination. As expected, no ACP was measured in both serum samples. Then, ACP solution of known concentration was spiked into the diluted serum sample to check the accuracy of the method. As exhibited in Table 2 , the spiked values match well with the measured values, with an average recovery varying from 95.0 to 108 % and a relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 2.0 %. All these acceptable results proclaim its application potential for the determination of ACP in real sample.

Due to spectral overlap, MnO2 NSs quenches the fluorescence of VB2 through IFE. ACP catalyzed hydrolysis of AAP generates AA that can decompose MnO2 NSs, resulting in the recovery of VB2 fluorescence. In the basis of these, a turn on fluorescence sensing technology was used for measuring ACP with VB2- MnO2 NSs as a probe. Under the conditions of pH 4.0, reaction time 5 min, MnO2 NSs 100 μM, and VB2 5.0 μM, the fluorescence recovery value at 527 nm (ΔF) enables the sensitive assay of ACP with comparable analytical performance to the reported methods in literature. The method has the advantages of high sensitivity, short response time, easy operation, and low cost of materials. Moreover, by combining oxidative MnO2 NSs with fluorescent VB2, a pioneering approach was established to determine other analytes using VB2 as the fluorescent reporting group, providing new ideas for exploring the application of MnO2 NSs and VB2 in fluorescence assay. The limitation of this method is that it requires the preparation of MnO2 NSs and is not suitable for continuous and in situ analysis.

Li Huang: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shangying Qin: Data curation, Investigation. Chuang Wen: Validation. Yuanjin Xu: Supervision. Zhongwei Lin: Software. Yilin Wang: Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (YCSW2023049).
